hypothesis: If language models for materials design are fine-tuned using Direct Preference Optimization guided by physics-based thermodynamic simulation feedback (rather than human or proxy rewards), then they will generate BCC/B2 superalloy compositions with higher reward scores, greater novelty, and broader coverage compared to supervised fine-tuning alone and prompt-based baselines, because iterative preference learning aligns the model's generative distribution toward physically valid, synthesis-relevant regions of compositional space that cannot be captured by training data or prompting strategies alone.
